content.text: What it is

Secrets rotation for LLM API keys is the practice of systematically replacing API keys used to authenticate against language model providers (such as Anthropic, OpenAI, Google, or self-hosted inference endpoints) on a regular schedule and whenever a potential compromise is suspected. Rather than using long-lived static keys, rotation ensures that any key exposure has a bounded validity window.

For agentic AI systems, LLM API keys are especially sensitive: they authorize inference requests that can consume large token budgets and may expose confidential prompts or tool outputs to third-party providers. A leaked LLM API key can result in direct financial harm (unauthorized usage charges), data exposure, and denial of service to legitimate agent operations.
Why it matters

LLM API keys are higher-value targets than typical service credentials because they carry implicit budget authorization. An attacker with a leaked Anthropic or OpenAI API key can run millions of tokens of inference against the legitimate account, incurring costs in the tens of thousands of dollars before rate limits or billing alerts trigger. Unlike a database password, which requires network access to the database to exploit, an LLM API key can be used from anywhere on the internet.

Regular rotation limits exposure to a bounded window. Automated rotation removes the human bottleneck and guarantees that rotation actually happens on schedule — a common failure mode when rotation is a manual process. Pairing rotation with audit logs and anomaly detection means that even if a key leaks, the window between leak and detection shrinks.
Benefits
Bounded exposure window.
A leaked key is only valid until the next rotation, not indefinitely. With 30-day rotation, maximum exposure is 30 days even if the leak goes undetected.
Compliance alignment.
SOC 2 and ISO 27001 controls require periodic credential rotation. Automated rotation provides evidence of compliance without manual effort.
Audit trail.
Rotation events, key usage patterns, and access logs are all recorded in the secret store's audit trail, enabling forensic analysis after an incident.
Reduced blast radius per key.
Using different keys per environment (dev, staging, production) and per service limits the impact of any single key compromise to one environment or service.
Trade-offs and limitations
Rotation downtime risk.

If services fetch the key at startup time only (not dynamically), rotating the key while the service is running causes authentication failures on the next restart. Zero-downtime rotation requires either a dynamic secret reload mechanism or a dual-key overlap period.
Not all providers support programmatic key creation.

Some LLM providers do not expose an API for key management, requiring manual key creation through a web console. In these cases, automation is partial: the storage and distribution steps can be automated, but the creation step remains manual.
Rotation does not stop a live attacker.

A key that is actively being used by an attacker at rotation time will continue working until it is explicitly revoked. Rotation schedules are not a substitute for anomaly detection and emergency revocation.
Key proliferation.

Using separate keys per environment and per service multiplies the number of keys to manage. A secret store and rotation automation are essential at this scale; spreadsheet-based key management is not viable.
How it works

Rotation follows a five-step pipeline: trigger, generate, store, reload, revoke. The trigger fires on a schedule (typically 30 days) or on demand when a compromise is suspected. The generation step calls the LLM provider's API to create a new key alongside the existing one — both keys are valid simultaneously during the overlap period. The new key is written to the secret store as a new version, preserving the old version for rollback.

The reload step triggers a rolling restart or a dynamic secret refresh across all agent service instances. Services pick up the new key from the store on restart or via a sidecar that watches the secret store for changes. During the overlap period (typically 15-30 minutes), both the old and new keys are valid at the provider, so in-flight requests using the old key complete successfully.

Once all service instances have confirmed they are using the new key (verified by checking that no requests are using the old key's identifier, if the provider supports key-level usage attribution), the old key is revoked at the provider. Revocation is the final step, not the first — premature revocation causes service disruptions.
Architecture

attrs.alt: LLM API key rotation pipeline: 5 steps (Schedule Trigger, Generate New Key at provider, Store in Vault as new version, Reload Services via rolling restart or dynamic reload, Revoke Old Key after drain). A dual-key overlap period of 15-30 minutes ensures no dropped requests. Three bottom boxes show Secret Store (versioned, audit-logged, AppRole access), Agent Services (startup fetch or sidecar injection), and Monitoring (alerts on key age, auth failure spike, and unexpected usage).

content.text: The secret store is the distribution hub. The rotation CI job drives the pipeline. Monitoring provides the feedback loop that detects rotation failures and post-rotation auth anomalies.
When to use it
Any production system that calls an LLM API.
Static long-lived keys with no rotation are a security risk regardless of system size.
Systems subject to SOC 2, PCI, or ISO 27001 controls.
These standards require evidence of credential rotation; automated rotation with audit logs satisfies this control.
After any suspected credential exposure.
If a key may have been logged, committed to source control, or transmitted insecurely, emergency rotation and immediate revocation should be performed within minutes.
When onboarding or offboarding engineers.
Rotate keys when someone who had access to secrets leaves the team, even if the departure is planned.
How to implement
Centralize key storage.

Move all LLM API keys from environment variable files, CI/CD secret stores, and developer laptops into a central secret store (HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, GCP Secret Manager). Never allow keys in source control.
Create one key per environment per service.
Do not share a single API key across production and staging, or across multiple agent services. This limits blast radius and enables per-service usage tracking.
Build the rotation pipeline.

A CI job (or Vault's dynamic secrets engine) handles key generation, storage, service reload triggering, and old key revocation. The pipeline should be idempotent and tested in a staging environment first.
Implement dynamic secret loading.

Agent services should refresh their LLM API key from the secret store on a short TTL (e.g., 5 minutes) rather than reading it only at startup. Use Vault Agent sidecar or a Kubernetes External Secrets operator for this.
Add rotation monitoring.

Alert when a key has not been rotated in more than 45 days, when a rotation pipeline fails, or when auth failures spike immediately after a rotation (indicating some services did not pick up the new key).
Document emergency rotation procedures.
On-call engineers must be able to perform an emergency rotation in under 15 minutes. Document the steps, including how to trigger an out-of-band rotation, in the incident runbook.
Examples
Automated rotation with Vault and Anthropic API

"""rotate_anthropic_key.py

Rotation script: creates a new Anthropic API key, stores it in Vault,
triggers a rolling restart of agent services, then revokes the old key.
Runs as a CI job every 30 days.
"""
import os
import httpx
import hvac  # HashiCorp Vault client

VAULT_ADDR = os.environ["VAULT_ADDR"]
VAULT_TOKEN = os.environ["VAULT_TOKEN"]
ANTHROPIC_ADMIN_KEY = os.environ["ANTHROPIC_ADMIN_KEY"]  # separate admin key for rotation only
SECRET_PATH = "secret/data/prod/anthropic"


def create_new_anthropic_key(name: str) -> str:
    """Create a new API key via the Anthropic admin API."""
    resp = httpx.post(
        "https://api.anthropic.com/v1/organizations/api_keys",
        headers={"x-api-key": ANTHROPIC_ADMIN_KEY, "anthropic-version": "2023-06-01"},
        json={"name": name, "permissions": ["model:invoke"]},
    )
    resp.raise_for_status()
    return resp.json()["secret_key"]


def store_in_vault(new_key: str, old_key: str) -> None:
    client = hvac.Client(url=VAULT_ADDR, token=VAULT_TOKEN)
    client.secrets.kv.v2.create_or_update_secret(
        path="prod/anthropic",
        secret={"api_key": new_key, "previous_api_key": old_key},
    )
    print("New key stored in Vault")


def get_current_key_from_vault() -> str:
    client = hvac.Client(url=VAULT_ADDR, token=VAULT_TOKEN)
    data = client.secrets.kv.v2.read_secret_version(path="prod/anthropic")
    return data["data"]["data"]["api_key"]


def trigger_rolling_restart(service: str) -> None:
    resp = httpx.post(
        f"https://deploy.internal/api/services/{service}/restart",
        headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {os.environ['DEPLOY_TOKEN']}"},
        json={"strategy": "rolling", "wait_for_healthy": True},
    )
    resp.raise_for_status()
    print(f"Rolling restart triggered for {service}")


def revoke_old_anthropic_key(key_id: str) -> None:
    resp = httpx.delete(
        f"https://api.anthropic.com/v1/organizations/api_keys/{key_id}",
        headers={"x-api-key": ANTHROPIC_ADMIN_KEY, "anthropic-version": "2023-06-01"},
    )
    resp.raise_for_status()
    print(f"Old key {key_id} revoked")


def main():
    import time
    old_key = get_current_key_from_vault()
    new_key = create_new_anthropic_key("prod-agent-rotated")
    store_in_vault(new_key, old_key)
    for service in ["summarizer-agent", "codereviewer-agent", "researcher-agent"]:
        trigger_rolling_restart(service)
    print("Waiting 20 minutes for overlap period before revoking old key...")
    time.sleep(20 * 60)
    # In production: verify no requests are using the old key before revoking
    revoke_old_anthropic_key(old_key_id="sk-ant-...")  # use key_id from creation response
    print("Rotation complete")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
Dynamic secret loading in agent service
Real-world example

A platform team operating a fleet of AI agents discovered that an engineer had accidentally committed an Anthropic API key in a test file that was briefly pushed to a public GitHub repository before being caught and deleted. The key had been live for 18 minutes before it was removed from the repository.

The team executed emergency rotation within 8 minutes: they generated a new key, stored it in Vault, triggered a rolling restart of all agent services, waited 15 minutes for the overlap period, and revoked the old key. Total downtime: zero (the overlap period covered the restart). They then found $340 in unauthorized inference charges that had occurred in those 18 minutes, confirmed the attacker's IP was distinct from their infrastructure, and filed a billing dispute with the provider.

Post-incident, they implemented pre-commit hooks to scan for API key patterns, moved to 14-day rotation cycles, and added a GitGuardian integration to detect future accidental key commits before they are pushed.
Common mistakes
Revoking the old key before all services have reloaded.
Services that read the key at startup only will fail with 401 errors after the old key is revoked. Always observe the overlap period.
Storing the rotation admin key alongside the service key.
The admin key used to create and revoke API keys must be stored separately with more restrictive access than the service keys it manages.
Logging the key value in rotation scripts.
Rotation scripts that print the key to stdout will expose it in CI/CD build logs. Log only the key ID or a masked representation.
Sharing one key across all environments.
A key used in development and production means a developer machine compromise also compromises production. Always use separate keys per environment.
No post-rotation monitoring.

A rotation that causes some services to fail silently (e.g., they log auth errors but do not page) will not be detected until users report degraded behavior. Alert on auth failure spikes in the 30 minutes after rotation.
Best practices
Rotate LLM API keys every 30 days and immediately on any suspected exposure.
Use a dual-key overlap period (at least 15 minutes) to prevent downtime during rotation.
Store keys in a central secret store with versioning, access control, and immutable audit logs.
Use separate keys per environment and per service to limit blast radius.
Implement dynamic secret loading so services pick up new keys without a full restart.
Never log or print key values; log only the key ID or a masked placeholder.
Alert on auth failure spikes in the 30 minutes after rotation and on keys older than 45 days.
Document emergency rotation procedures and test them quarterly.
Performance and scalability

The rotation pipeline itself is not in the hot path and can run as a scheduled CI job. The key distribution step — services refreshing from the secret store — should use a short TTL (5 minutes) with in-process caching so the store is not called on every inference request. At high request volumes, reading from Vault on every LLM call would create a performance bottleneck; the local cache is essential.

Security and reliability

The rotation pipeline itself must be secured as carefully as the keys it manages. The admin key used to create and revoke service keys must have more restrictive access controls than the service keys. Store the admin key in a hardware security module (HSM) or a Vault instance with strict access logging. Rotation CI jobs should run in isolated environments with short-lived credentials and should not have access to production data beyond what is needed to complete the rotation.

For reliability, the pipeline must be idempotent: if a rotation fails halfway through (e.g., the old key is revoked but some services did not reload), re-running the pipeline must produce a consistent state without double-revocation errors or service outages. Keep both the current and previous key version in the secret store at all times to enable rollback.
Key takeaways
LLM API keys are high-value targets because they carry budget authorization and can be exploited from anywhere on the internet without network access to internal systems.
Automated 30-day rotation limits maximum exposure to a bounded window; manual rotation is unreliable because it is consistently deprioritized under feature pressure.
A dual-key overlap period (15-30 minutes) prevents downtime during rotation by allowing both old and new keys to be valid simultaneously while services reload.
Dynamic secret loading (5-minute TTL with local cache) allows services to pick up new keys without a full restart and without per-request secret store calls.
Use separate keys per environment and per service to limit blast radius; a dev machine compromise must not expose production LLM access.
Never log key values; log only key IDs. Scan CI/CD pipelines and git history for accidental key exposure with tools like GitGuardian or truffleHog.
Alert on auth failure spikes in the 30 minutes after rotation; a rotation that leaves some services using a revoked key causes silent failures.
